one_line_takeaway: Rewriting a teacher LLM's reasoning traces with an LLM-optimized natural-language instruction (rather than sampling tricks or post-training) simultaneously degrades unauthorized-distillation student accuracy by up to 61.3% and embeds a reliably detectable, near-zero-false-alarm ownership watermark, without hurting -- and often improving -- the teacher's own accuracy.
key_finding: The optimized-prompting (OPT) instruction-based method was the strongest anti-distillation approach: on GSM8K it reduced student accuracy by 24.2% (Llama-3.2-1B), 49.1% (Llama-3.2-3B), and 61.3% (Qwen2.5-1.5B) relative to clean-trace distillation, while simultaneously raising teacher accuracy by about +2.9% (from 88.8% baseline). On MATH, OPT reduced student accuracy by 2.9%, 7.2%, and 30.5% for the three students respectively while raising teacher accuracy by about +22.3% (from a 64.8% baseline) -- the paper attributes teacher improvement to the rewriter correcting errors in the original traces. Prompt-based approaches outperformed all gradient-based variants (FO-Grad, HB-Grad, RHB-Grad), which the authors note as an open, unexplained limitation. Against baselines ADS (Antidistillation Sampling) and DOGe, OPT achieved both stronger anti-distillation and higher teacher accuracy; ADS and DOGe degraded the teacher's own performance (e.g., DOGe -44.4% and ADS -25.2% teacher accuracy loss on GSM8K for one student configuration) and both baselines' outputs showed degenerate token repetition (DOGe perplexity 1.42 vs. original 2.33; LLM-judge naturalness 2.03 for DOGe and 2.31 for ADS vs. 4.12 original). For API watermarking, the method achieved near-perfect true-detection with essentially zero false-alarm rate using as few as K=5 verification queries on GSM8K (Llama-3.1-8B: TD 1.00/FA 0.02 at K=5/20; Qwen2.5-1.5B: TD 0.98/FA 0.00), outperforming He et al. 2022a (high TD but FA up to 0.99), GINSEW and KGW (near-zero TD), and the strongest baseline VIA, achieving near-perfect detection at only K=1 while VIA's detection rate remained ~30% at that K. Both anti-distillation and watermarking effects survived adaptive-distiller countermeasures: paraphrasing and KPOD did not recover student accuracy (and sometimes amplified degradation), and the watermark remained detectable under regex filtering, paraphrasing, and CDG-KD, though these adaptive attacks did cause additional student accuracy loss as a side effect.
core_contribution: The paper proposes 'trace rewriting': a unified framework where a teacher LLM's reasoning traces (used for knowledge-distillation training data) are dynamically rewritten, query-by-query, by an assistant LLM (or via gradient-based perturbation) before being returned to a caller. The rewriting serves two distinct IP-protection goals: (1) anti-distillation -- degrade a downstream student model's ability to learn from the traces while preserving/improving the teacher's own answer accuracy, and (2) API watermarking -- covertly embed a 'trigger = target' behavioral association into the traces so that a student fine-tuned on them can later be interrogated to prove unauthorized distillation, with low false-alarm rate. Two instruction-based rewriting variants are introduced: semantic prompting (a fixed natural-language rewrite instruction) and optimized prompting (an OPRO-style search over rewrite instructions scored by accuracy drop on proxy student models). Three gradient-based variants operating in token-embedding space are also introduced: first-order (FO-Grad), Hessian-based (HB-Grad, approximating the poisoning-attack implicit-function-theorem gradient without inverting the full Hessian), and a robust variant (RHB-Grad) that adds Gaussian noise to proxy-student parameters before computing the gradient; a HotFlip-style discrete token-substitution variant is also described in the appendix. All rewriting methods mask the final answer token span so correctness is preserved.
threat_model: Setting: a teacher LLM T is queried sequentially/online by a potential distiller (the 'student' developer) via black-box API access; the teacher provider can only modify its own generated response traces (it cannot see or control the full query dataset, cannot inject extra training examples, and has no control over student architecture, hyperparameters, or training procedure). The threat is unauthorized single-source distillation, e.g., someone using API access to a frontier reasoning model (the paper names ChatGPT and Claude as examples of models an unauthorized distiller might target) to train a cheaper student model on the teacher's reasoning traces via supervised fine-tuning (SFT). Constraints imposed on the defense: rewritten traces must preserve answer correctness and semantic coherence/fluency (ruling out injecting nonsense tokens or trivially detectable noise), since an adaptive distiller could otherwise filter such traces. The paper additionally evaluates robustness against several adaptive-distiller countermeasures: paraphrasing (Parrot paraphraser) before fine-tuning, KPOD (keypoint-based progressive CoT distillation reweighting), regex-based token filtering around watermark trigger characters, and CDG-KD (contrastive-decoding-guided distillation designed to scrub token-level statistical watermarks).
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: (1) the gradient-based rewriting approaches are computationally expensive (Hessian computation, iterative updates) and are empirically less effective than the prompt-based methods, with the cause of this gap left uninvestigated; (2) the optimized-prompting search relies on an ensemble of proxy student models to score candidate rewrite instructions, and while an ensemble is used to mitigate overfitting, transfer to unknown real-world student architectures is not guaranteed; (3) experiments focus solely on SFT-based distillation -- efficacy against other distillation techniques (e.g., distribution-matching or RL-based distillation) is left to future work.

datasets_used: MMLU (supplement only, not verified in extracted text)
MMLU-Pro (supplement only, not verified in extracted text)
quantitative_results: OPT anti-distillation on GSM8K: student accuracy reduction of -24.2% (Llama-3.2-1B), -49.1% (Llama-3.2-3B), -61.3% (Qwen2.5-1.5B) vs clean distillation; teacher accuracy +2.9% relative to 88.8% clean baseline
OPT anti-distillation on MATH: student accuracy reduction of -2.9% (Llama-3.2-1B), -7.2% (Llama-3.2-3B), -30.5% (Qwen2.5-1.5B); teacher accuracy +22.3% relative to 64.8% clean baseline

Watermarking on GSM8K (Table 1), teacher clean accuracy 88.76%, method teacher accuracy 90.98%: TD/FA at K=5/K=20 -- Llama-3.1-8B 1.00/1.00 TD, 0.00/0.02 FA; Llama-3.2-3B 0.55/0.99 TD, 0.00/0.00 FA; Qwen2.5-1.5B 0.98/1.00 TD, 0.00/0.00 FA
Trace-quality perplexity (Llama-3.1-8B reference, MATH n=150): Original 2.33, Semantic 4.24, Optimized 3.79, ADS 2.31, DOGe 1.42
LLM-as-judge overall quality (1-5, MATH n=150): Original 4.01, Semantic 3.09, Optimized 3.83, ADS 2.71, DOGe 2.40
baselines_compared: Antidistillation Sampling (ADS, Savani et al. 2025) -- anti-distillation baseline
DOGe / Defensive Output Generation (Li et al. 2025) -- anti-distillation baseline
He et al. 2022a lexical/synonym watermarking -- watermarking baseline
GINSEW (Zhao et al. 2023b) sinusoidal signal watermarking -- watermarking baseline
KGW (Kirchenbauer et al. 2023) token-bias watermarking -- watermarking baseline
VIA / Virus Infection Attack (Liang et al. 2025) -- watermarking baseline
